item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love the random scp function for helping me find gems like Anne here. Sarah definitely gets my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: very good!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can blame Buttersafe, but when I see the title I immediately think "Doo-Doo Butthole".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happy +100

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What will happen we call it until names in caller's language run out? he can't call it more?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Brilliant wordplay! Thanks for reminding us all that SCPs can be nice and mental effects don't have to result in copious amounts of dead researchers.

I hope Rosie grows okay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just belatedly discovered this little gem, and I like it a lot. Feels like R&R after all these nightmarish monsters, events, phenomenons and threats.

I only grokked on 'a rose by any other name' when I read the comments, and it makes the skip even better, IMO. What I did grok was 1) a fun and bold attempt to circumvent the 'Foundation only refers to SCPs by their number, never by any name or nickname', and 2) It is commonly accepted that naming anything makes you care about it more as the conclusion states. BTW, I Wonder if the skip would be better without spelling the latter out completely? Let the reader think of this on their own?
Oh, and 3), related to 2) I am used to giving A LOT of different names to things/creatures/people I love. Especially my cats. Until recently I had three cats, and each of them had dozens of names, plus names I indifferently gave any of them, plus collective names for the set. This skip is an interesting echo of that!

Anyway, good work, fun, enjoyable, thought-provoking, with an underlying dark leitmotiv: what exactly happened to the old lady and to her garden? Is Dr. ██████ in danger? What about his assistants? Perhaps Geneviève has some hidden, time-bomb extra power…?

Comfortable, smiling +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh wow, this is really recent - I've been reading through in order over the past couple of years during my lunch breaks and this is the newest post I've come across :O

Anyway, sweet idea, though I wonder if they would have used the names as much in the document once they knew the effect… also, could be interesting to see what happens when they 'run out' of names xD

+1 in any case :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like Natziri! Now you can see the translation of Xóchitl on Spanish here http://lafundacionscp.wikidot.com/scp-1785

And my 1+
Perhaps it was confusion with not getting feedback before posting and then only expecting feedback from the article's discussion. Anyway, congrats on +50!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's only because I'm an idiot, and I mean SCP-2602!

Now edited in the post above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think he thinks that because it's discouraged during contests. BTW, if you're ever in a contest, editing after the winners have been announced is fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure why you'd think that; editing after posting is absolutely fine. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know editing after posting is frowned upon

I will have to disagree on that notion. It is just us using the medium to our advantage, that we can afford to modify and improve on the work over time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since a few more people have mentioned the formatting error, I've moved it to the bottom of the description. I know editing after posting is frowned upon but considering it's the same text, just scooted to where it's supposed to be, I figure it's fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very creative and highly amusing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
More lovable than the mask.

Now, to not mention that old thing, I'm a big fan of what Esmerelda has going on here. No need for a background exploring what Jessamine's effects resulted from, as the article itself is rather neat as a standalone with no lengthy supplement provided.

Reminds me of a series one in that regard, and recognizing the effect before I read it made me smile.

+1, a nice first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will not fault the article for it definitely shows the anomaly. However, the presentation here does diminish the format of an executive report of an anomalous object to be contained.

And I do agree that discovery should be in the later half. I think it does affect comprehension of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was wary of this format screw at first, but it quickly grew on me. The article was entertaining, and the diminishing returns aspect in the addenda was a nice addition. I do agree with Decibelles that the discovery log should not be at the beginning of the description, but rather at the bottom, as the first and foremost thing that needs to be in the description section is the physical description of the object and its anomaly.

Still, when all is said and done, its worth an upvote.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really, really not seeing the 2612 comparison here.

item.title: Did you know that this rosebush used to be a library?
item.content:encoded: I found it funny for the same reason the damnable library skip was enjoyable. I upvoted, but I may rescind at a later date, depending on how I feel about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. The priority of the description should be to tell you what the SCP is so you can contain it as fast as possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just to elaborate on a couple points…

I did have the intent of the old woman's age and dementia being the source of the effect, hence the mention of a medical record

I probably wasn't very clear here. My issue with this part, or a big part of it at least, was simply that the article seemed to be presenting it as a matter of old age = dementia, rather than stating she had dementia. The distinction is relevant and fairly important.

Regarding order of history and description: forestalling an explanation is a valid way to build suspense, but I don't think that's what this article operates on, and switching the order of those two things isn't a very good way to go about it even if it were. An oft-repeated interpretation of SCP articles themselves is that they're executive summaries of things in the Foundation's possession or sphere of awareness: that you should be able to look at one fairly quickly and ascertain what you're dealing with - say, if alarm klaxons start blaring and you're handed a folder. It doesn't make sense to forestall the relevant part, an explanation of what something is, for the presentation of backstory that's probably not relevant to the here-and-now. The history is well and good, but generally speaking, it shouldn't go first. It's entirely possible to write an article that flips that on its head, but this isn't it, and doesn't need to be. I appreciate the understatement, here; part of why catching onto the quote-play disappointed me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did have the intent of the old woman's age and dementia being the source of the effect, hence the mention of a medical record, but wasn't sure how to tie it in and decided that it was better left as loosely implied. You were able to extrapolate it, so the writing is definitely on the wall, but I do agree I should've found a way to commit to it more solidly.

I was also unaware that history shouldn't precede the meat of the description, I was under the impression it was alright for the sake of delaying the "reveal" of the item's anomalous properties. Just you and one other person have mentioned it so I don't think it's a commonly known rule, but I'll keep it in mind for future attempts.

I'm sorry to hear that your interest in the article waned when you knew it was a literature reference, but thank you for the critique. (and thank you to everyone else that's taken the time to comment and rate it!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
History should never come before the description. There is virtually never going to be a situation wherein how something was found is more important, pressing information than what something is and does, or the reason(s) it's an SCP, especially not something as ostensibly innocuous as this.

I was initially amused by this, above complaint notwithstanding. Then, I remembered the "rose by any other name" line, and now it strikes me as a less creative, more on-the-nose toaster. My initial thought was that this was intended to dove-tail with the original caretaker's dementia (which, if it existed, should be specified - old age does not necessarily entail it, and something like referring to a single plant by a different name every time would suggest it), whether caused by, causing, or simply related to it, but that gets overshadowed by the fact that the author was actually just playing on a famous quote.

This has a vaguely insidious quality, considering the apparent mental state of the original caretaker, personnel who are apparently not eccentric old ladies developing unusual feelings of fondness for it, and the fact that, if I'm reading right, it was the only thing in the garden.

But, again, that all gets overshadowed for me by the fact that it's just a rosebush by a different name every time. Would this work, if it were a different plant? Who knows. I'd probably prefer it that way; I liked it until I saw what you did here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely did not get the 'by any other name' theme until I got to the comments. Even so, quite enjoyable in a quiet, understated way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The rose bush might have thorns, but the story doesn't seem to. The basic idea of disassociating the signifier from the signified (as in SCP-426, SCP-1539 or SCP-2719), or more simply by creating an anomaly that affects the manner by which it is referred (as in SCP-2602) is a well-travelled path at this stage.

This iteration takes the basic idea and doesn't really go anywhere with it. The first three paragraphs (before the anomaly is described) are painfully obvious in existing only to exhibit the anomaly before it is described, by way of a hook. But once the anomaly is known, there is nothing to follow. The generational addendum provides no surprises or added complexity - the effect simply fades away, meaning that the story does too. The examples cited above each uses the semiotic or semantic trickery as a way to introduce a frisson of horror or some intellectual games (or both), but this just states a very simple effect and lets it sit there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really care for the basis of this. Taking a phrase (and in the most literal sense at that) and beating it into the ground in the most direct manner possible isn't doing a whole lot for me. And because this is based around something so thin, the extended description of what happens when you breed this thing, bring in non-English speakers, etc. only serves to reinforce what we've already been told in the description, making the article drag.

There's also a few little things that are kind of dissonant. How is a single rose plant in someone's yard being left behind after an estate sale going to attract the attention of a nation-state level shadow organization? Why is the number of months before the secondary properties were discovered a privileged piece of information? And how incompetent is this iteration of the Foundation that it's letting researchers keep anomalous phenomena as office decorations?

I'm not sure if I would have enjoyed this had it kept any of the subtext of the original "rose by any other name" quotation, but something like that might have at least had some thematic heft to it. This just feels insubstantial to me, and it's not weird or mind-breaking enough to generate interest just by its very existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A man who would tell a pun would pick a pocket; Shakespearean reference notwithstanding. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I balked at first, but was won over when I realized that all the names were referring to the same rose bush.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This fits in neatly with other similar format screws, from the toaster on down, but the diminishing returns effect makes it stand apart. There's potential here to draw a lot of emotion from an extended narrative, something along the lines of that one guy in the Japanese fishing village, but as it stands, it's not bad, and worth an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm. I dunno, this kinda just feels like an anomalous item with a bit more expansion. It almost heads the direction of SCP-426, but doesn't really take any other turn except for the name-thing. I feel as if I would be equally satisfied with what it is if I'd just read it as a single sentence; doesn't appear to need any more than that to me.

Pretty decent for a first try, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a really cool scip! I love how it takes the saying to another level. Very creative. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really awesome for a first skip! Keep up the good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it a lot, but always balk at discovery at the top of the description. Discovery of an SCP just does not work when it's at the top of the description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shit, Corny. I didn't know you were an SCPer, too, now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can already see Vivian becoming a fan favourite. The article is simple, has a very neat effect, and gives the reader just enough information for them to have their own stories of Carina. Good work, and congrats on your first article being successful +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Matilda makes a nice addition to the Series 32 roster, I noticed that Cindy went up by 3 votes in the time it took me to read and cast mine. I think Isabelle is gonna do just fine. +1

ETA: Wrong number! Not used to seeing new skips outside the 2000 range.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was hoping to take this spot….

That aside, while this feels like a good SCP, It didn't stand out to me. It matches the look and feel of the older scips, but it didn't pique my interest.

Positively inclined neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is bizarre as hell… I like it.
Serena is my favorite. :P
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My first skip, I hope you all enjoy this exploration of taking the phrase 'a rose by any other name' entirely too far.
